---
title: "6.2 Edge cases and failure modes"
---

## Explanation

An edge case is an input near a boundary of the specification. A failure mode
is a way the code or method can give a wrong, misleading, or unusable result.

Edge cases and failure modes are not only programming details. In scientific
computing, the code can run without an error and still fail scientifically:
the result may not support the claim being made.

It is useful to separate three levels:

- the program crashes or throws an error,
- the program returns a numerically wrong answer,
- the answer is numerically plausible but scientifically misleading.

For an average function, edge cases include an empty array, a one-element array,
negative values, repeated values, very large values, and missing or invalid
values. Failure modes include dividing by zero, silently ignoring invalid data,
or computing an ordinary average when the specification required a weighted
average.

For root finding, edge cases include an interval where the function does not
change sign, a discontinuity, multiple roots, a flat derivative, and a root near
the boundary of the interval. Failure modes include reporting convergence to
the wrong root, stopping because the step size is small even though the
residual is not small, or trusting a result without checking assumptions of the
method.

For Monte Carlo simulation, edge cases include zero samples, very small sample
counts, invalid parameters, a fixed random seed, and repeated independent runs.
Failure modes include insufficient burn-in, strong autocorrelation, a missing
random seed, too few independent samples, or drawing a physical conclusion from
noise.

A compact way to organize this thinking is a table:

| Task | Edge case | Possible failure mode | Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average | Empty input | Division by zero or meaningless result | Throw a clear error |
| Root finding | No sign change | Method assumption is violated | Check preconditions |
| Monte Carlo | Too few samples | Plausible but noisy estimate | Compare multiple seeds |

## Things to look up

- Edge case
- Failure mode
- Boundary condition
- Invalid input
- Defensive programming
- Convergence criterion
- Burn-in
- Autocorrelation

## Exercise

For each task, make a table with at least three edge cases, two failure modes,
and one check that could detect each failure mode:

- computing an average,
- finding a root of a function,
- running a Monte Carlo simulation.

## Notes for the exercise

- Include both programming failures and scientific failures.
- Do not list only syntax errors.
- Include cases where the code runs but the result is misleading.
- For root finding, mention at least one assumption of the method.
- For Monte Carlo, mention randomness, sample size, and independence.
- Prefer checks that can run quickly before a long calculation.
